titleEn: UC Riverside Develops Modern-Day Robotic Divining Rod
titleZh: 加州大学河滨分校开发出现代版机器人探水杖：逐树精准灌溉
summaryZh: 加州大学河滨分校（UC Riverside）研究团队开发出一套新型土壤湿度测绘系统，利用机器人测量土壤电导率，结合固定传感器数据，构建统计模型预测整个果园的含水量，实现逐棵树的精准灌溉。该系统解决了传统传感器成本高、覆盖有限的问题，帮助农民在干旱加剧的背景下避免过度灌溉，提升水资源利用效率，对果树种植业具有重要实践价值。
translationZh:
先进技术可以帮助农民解决一个日益严重的问题——在干旱和水资源短缺加剧的时代，过度灌溉。加州大学河滨分校（UC Riverside）开发的一套新系统能够逐棵树绘制土壤湿度地图，使种植者只在需要的地方和时间进行灌溉。

该系统由精准农业副教授、UCR农业、食品与环境中心（CAFE）主任埃利亚·斯库迪耶罗（Elia Scudiero）的研究团队领导，相关成果发表在《计算机与电子农业》（Computer and Electronics in Agriculture）期刊上。

水资源管理是加州及其他干旱地区农业面临的最大挑战之一。目前，一些种植者依赖埋在地下的土壤湿度传感器来决定何时灌溉。这些传感器价格昂贵，通常只安装在少数几个位置，导致种植者需要猜测数百或数千棵树之间的土壤条件差异。

“这些传感器提供的信息非常有限，”斯库迪耶罗说，“它们只能告诉你传感器所在位置附近的情况。”

即使喷灌系统在整个果园中均匀供水，同一地块内不同位置的土壤湿度及其对树木的可利用性也可能存在巨大差异。原因之一是土壤质地。充满细小颗粒的细质土壤能牢牢锁住水分，因为其表面积更大，水分可以附着其上。沙质土壤含有较大的颗粒和较少的细小颗粒，这使得水分流失更快。这些差异可能导致相邻的树木经历截然不同的条件。

新系统用详细的地图取代了有限的传感器数据和猜测。一台机器人在果园中移动，测量土壤的一种特性——电导率。这些读数与已埋入地下的固定湿度传感器的数据相结合，使研究人员能够建立一个统计模型，预测整个田地的含水量。

电导率表示电流在土壤中流动的容易程度，受水分、盐分和黏土含量等因素影响。通过将这些测量值与埋入式传感器的直接水分读数配对，系统可以将电导率转化为准确的土壤湿度估算值。结果就是逐棵树的湿度分布图。

“使用这种方法，种植者最终将知道他们有多少水，需要多少水，并且可以在特定树木缺水时对其进行灌溉，”斯库迪耶罗说。

保持适当的湿度水平对植物健康至关重要。水分过少的树木会处于胁迫状态，更容易受到病虫害的侵袭。然而，水分过多会使土壤孔隙充满水而非空气，从而剥夺根系的氧气。“存在一个最佳点，”他说。

更高的精准度还可以防止果园倒闭。
featuredReason: 该技术将机器人、传感器与统计建模结合，实现了果树种植中前所未有的逐棵精准灌溉，对智慧畜牧行业（尤其是种植-养殖结合场景）的水资源管理和自动化决策具有重要借鉴意义。
contentFull: By UC Riverside Share Advanced technology can help farmers get to the root of a growing problem — overwatering in an era of increasing drought and water scarcity. A new UC Riverside system can map soil moisture tree by tree, so growers water only where and when it’s needed. This system, detailed in the journal Computer and Electronics in Agriculture, was led by the research group of Elia Scudiero, associate professor of precision agriculture and the Director of UCR’s Center for Agriculture, Food, and the Environment (CAFE). Water management is one of the biggest challenges facing agriculture in California and other dry regions. Currently, some growers rely on soil moisture sensors buried in the ground to determine when to irrigate. These sensors are expensive and typically installed in only a few locations, leaving growers to guess how conditions vary across hundreds or thousands of trees. “The information those sensors provide is very limited,” Scudiero said. “It really only tells you what’s happening in the immediate areas where they’re placed.” Even when sprinkler systems deliver the same amount of water throughout an orchard, the soil moisture and its availability to trees can vary greatly from spot to spot within a single field. Top Articles Read More Read More Read More Read More Read More Beyond Disruption: Earning Trust and Driving Impact in AgTech Through Grower-Led Innovation One reason is soil texture. Fine soils packed with tiny particles hold water tightly because they have more surface area where water can cling. Sandy soils contain larger particles and fewer small ones, which allows water to drain more quickly. These differences can leave neighboring trees experiencing very different conditions. The new system replaces limited sensor data and guesswork with detailed maps. A robot moves through an orchard measuring a property of the soil called electrical conductivity. These readings, combined with data from the fixed moisture sensors already in the ground, allow researchers to build a statistical model that predicts water content across the entire field. Electrical conductivity indicates how easily electricity moves through the soil and is influenced by factors including moisture as well as salt and clay content. By pairing those measurements with direct water readings from buried sensors, the system can translate conductivity into accurate estimates of soil moisture. The result is a tree-by-tree picture of water distribution. “Using this method, growers will finally know how much water they have, and how much they need, and can water specific trees if they’re dry,” Scudiero said. Maintaining the right moisture level is important for plant health. Trees that receive too little water become stressed, and more vulnerable to pests and disease. Too much water, however, can deprive roots of oxygen as soil pores fill with water rather than air. “There’s a sweet spot,” he said. Enhanced precision could also keep orchards from folding. Growers already face tightening regulations on groundwater use while water costs continue to rise. “If water becomes limited, farmers have two choices,” Scudiero said. “They can retire orchards, or they can find ways to produce the same crops using less water.” The technology may also reduce fertilizer pollution. When fields are overwatered, nutrients applied to crops can wash below the root zone and into groundwater, polluting it. “If you apply only the amount of water the plants actually need, you reduce the risk of washing those nutrients away from the roots of the crops and into the environment,” Scudiero said. This project has been years in the making. Researchers began developing it in 2019 through collaborations between agricultural scientists and engineers at CAFE. For Scudiero, it represents the realization of a long-standing goal. He has studied soil conductivity technology for about 15 years and had hoped to someday pair it with autonomous vehicles capable of surveying entire fields. The team has already filed a patent related to how the robot interacts with sensors without disturbing their measurements. This research was conducted at the UCR Citrus Research Center & Agricultural Experiment Station. Future work will focus on testing the system with commercial growers beyond the university’s research orchards. Moving from research plots to real farms will require rugged machines capable of operating in all weather conditions and across different crop systems. Private industry partners may eventually adapt the technology into commercial products. The work is part of broader efforts at UCR to further the field of precision agriculture, where researchers are developing technologies that combine robotics, sensors, and data science to help farmers manage resources more efficiently. For growing facing limited water supplies, the payoff for this research could be significant. “More crop per drop!” Scudiero said. 0 1 5 UC Riverside Develops Modern-Day Robotic Divining Rod Subscribe Today For Subscribe The University of California, Riverside is a doctoral research university, a living laboratory for groundbreaking exploration of issues critical to Inland Southern California, the state and communities around the world. See all author stories here.
author: Matt Hopkins
contentHtml:
A new UC Riverside system can map soil moisture tree by tree, so growers water only where and when it’s needed.
The post UC Riverside Develops Modern-Day Robotic Divining Rod appeared first on Global Ag Tech Initiative.
